name: GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark
tagline: OpenAI's first real-time coding model, running on Cerebras at 1,000+ tokens/sec.
metaDescription: GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark is OpenAI's first real-time coding model, a smaller GPT-5.3-Codex variant running on Cerebras at 1,000+ tokens/sec with a 128k context wind…
overview: GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark is OpenAI's first model built specifically for real-time coding, released on February 12, 2026. It is a smaller, faster version of GPT-5.3-Codex, tuned so that targeted edits, logic changes, and interface tweaks return near-instantly rather than after the multi-second pause typical of a full agentic coding model. At launch it is text-only with a 128k-token context window.

What makes GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark distinctive is its serving stack. It is the first OpenAI production model to run outside Nvidia hardware, served on Cerebras' Wafer Scale Engine 3, and OpenAI calls it the first milestone in its partnership with Cerebras. On that ultra-low-latency hardware, Spark streams more than 1,000 tokens per second while staying capable on real-world coding tasks, making the model feel conversational inside an interactive edit loop.

GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark launched as a research preview for ChatGPT Pro subscribers, available in the latest Codex app, the Codex CLI (model id gpt-5.3-codex-spark), and the VS Code extension. It is not available in the OpenAI API at launch, and because it runs on specialized hardware its usage is governed by a separate, demand-adjusted rate limit. OpenAI positions Spark as a daily productivity driver for rapid prototyping and iterative front-end work, with the heavier GPT-5.3-Codex still recommended for long, multi-step architecture and stateful debugging.
strengths: Streams 1,000+ tokens per second on Cerebras WSE-3 hardware, making interactive edit loops feel near-instant
Purpose-built for real-time coding: targeted edits, reshaping logic, and refining interfaces with immediate feedback
First OpenAI production model served off Nvidia hardware, on a single wafer-scale chip with 4T+ transistors
Carries the same safety training as OpenAI's mainline models, including cyber-relevant training
OpenAI states it produces more capable responses than GPT-5.1-Codex-mini on SWE-Bench Pro and Terminal-Bench 2.0
useCases: Rapid prototyping and iterative front-end work where you tweak and immediately re-run
Making small, targeted code edits and refactors inside the Codex app, CLI, or VS Code
Real-time pair-style coding where low latency matters more than long-horizon planning
Quick interface and logic refinements during an interactive build loop

links.label: A new version of OpenAI's Codex is powered by a new dedicated chip (TechCrunch)
OpenAI released GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark, a real-time coding model (Help Net Security)

architecture: A smaller, distilled variant of GPT-5.3-Codex optimized for ultra-low-latency inference. It is the first OpenAI production model served outside the Nvidia GPU ecosystem, running on Cerebras' Wafer Scale Engine 3 (WSE-3), a single dinner-plate-sized chip with more than 4 trillion transistors and the largest on-chip memory of any AI processor. OpenAI states it carries the same safety training as its mainline models, including cyber-relevant training. OpenAI has not published a parameter count, knowledge cutoff, or max-output figure for the model.

faq.a: GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark is OpenAI's first model built specifically for real-time coding, released on February 12, 2026. It is a smaller, faster variant of GPT-5.3-Codex, optimized for near-instant, targeted edits inside an interactive coding loop. At launch it is text-only with a 128k-token context window.

When served on ultra-low-latency hardware, GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark streams more than 1,000 tokens per second. It achieves this by running on Cerebras' Wafer Scale Engine 3, making it the first OpenAI production model to run outside the Nvidia GPU ecosystem.

At launch it is a research preview for ChatGPT Pro subscribers, available in the Codex app, the Codex CLI (model id gpt-5.3-codex-spark), and the VS Code extension. It is not available in the OpenAI API at launch, and its usage is governed by a separate, demand-adjusted rate limit because it runs on specialized hardware.

faq.q: Should I use GPT-5.3-Codex-Spark or GPT-5.3-Codex?
faq.a: Use Spark for rapid, interactive work like targeted edits, prototyping, and iterative front-end loops where low latency matters most. For longer, multi-step architecture and stateful debugging, the heavier GPT-5.3-Codex remains the stronger choice. OpenAI says Spark produces more capable responses than GPT-5.1-Codex-mini on SWE-Bench Pro and Terminal-Bench 2.0.
